abstract: This conceptual paper argues that the 'social media' paradigm—organized around user-generated content circulating through social networks—is unraveling and must be replaced by a new analytical framework the authors call 'post-social media studies.' Törnberg and Rogers identify three interlocking dynamics driving this transformation: a shift from social-graph to interest-based algorithmic recommendation, the rise of generative AI as a producer of synthetic content, and a retreat from public platforms into private, bounded spaces. They propose that these dynamics are giving rise to three distinct emergent formations—algorithmic broadcasting platforms, semi-private spheres and micro-communities, and AI-mediated communication—each requiring new conceptual vocabularies, methods, and normative frameworks. Note: the title provided ('When do parties lie? Misinformation and radical-right populism across 26 countries') does not match the actual content of the supplied PDF, which is 'Towards a Post-Social Media Studies' by Petter Törnberg and Richard Rogers.
key_claims: The 'social media' paradigm is losing its adequacy as a unifying framework for understanding digital communication.

Three interrelated dynamics are dissolving the social media paradigm: an algorithmic shift from social graphs to interest-based recommendation, the introduction of generative AI as a substitute for user-generated content, and an exodus from public platforms into private spaces.
These shifts produce a new platform subject—the 'user-spectator' or 'algorithmic viewer'—whose primary mode of engagement is passive watching rather than active participation.
Three distinct post-social formations are emerging: algorithmic broadcasting platforms, semi-private spheres/micro-communities, and AI-mediated communication as a media form in its own right.
AI chatbots constitute a media form without publics: communication becomes individualized, generative, and ephemeral, leaving no shared text or public trace.
Existing conceptual tools (networked publics, participatory culture) and methodological approaches (network analysis, API-based scraping) are inadequate for studying these new formations.
These transformations erode the shared epistemic infrastructure on which democratic communication has historically depended, concentrating communicative power in new ways.
methods: Conceptual and theoretical synthesis rather than original empirical demonstration.
Literature review integrating platform studies, communication theory, digital sociology, and human–machine communication scholarship.

Illustrative use of secondary evidence, including Meta's antitrust court filings (showing 7% of Instagram and 17% of Facebook time spent on followed content), industry developments (TikTok's For You Page, Meta and YouTube reorientations), and survey statistics on AI chatbot adoption versus social media posting.
Comparative analytical framework that distinguishes three emergent post-social formations and maps distinct research challenges to each.
findings: Major platforms have shifted from social-graph-based distribution to algorithmic recommendation organized around inferred interest and passive attention signals (watch time, dwell time, completion rate).
User participation on legacy public platforms has declined significantly (cited ~50% drop in posting/viewing on Facebook and Twitter/X between 2020 and 2024).
AI chatbot use (52% of U.S. adults in 2025) now exceeds posting activity on major social media platforms (35% Facebook, 20% Instagram, 11% Twitter/X).
Generative AI enables platforms to populate feeds with synthetic content (e.g., 'Shrimp Jesus' phenomenon), reducing dependence on user-generated content.
Communicative practices are migrating toward semi-private spheres (messaging groups, Substack, invite-only communities) organized around trust and reciprocity rather than visibility and scale.

Each emergent formation poses distinct research challenges: algorithmic opacity for broadcasting platforms, access and ethics for semi-private spheres, and the absence of shared texts/publics for AI-mediated communication.
contributions: Proposes 'post-social media studies' as a new analytical framework and research agenda for digital communication.
Articulates a typology of three emergent post-social formations (algorithmic broadcasting platforms, semi-private spheres, AI-mediated communication).
Introduces and synthesizes concepts such as 'algorithmic publics,' 'imitation publics,' and the 'user-spectator' to replace 'networked publics.'

Outlines methodological reorientations needed for each formation—e.g., flow analysis and rhythmanalysis for broadcasting platforms, ethnographic and design-analytic methods for semi-private spheres, and AI auditing for chatbot-mediated communication.
Frames the normative and democratic stakes of communicative infrastructures that no longer reliably produce shared publics or stable texts.
framing: The paper positions itself within platform studies, digital communication research, and critical theory of media infrastructures, engaging traditions of networked publics (boyd, Papacharissi), platformization (van Dijck, Helmond), surveillance capitalism (Zuboff), and emerging human–machine communication scholarship (Guzman & Lewis). It advances a strong programmatic claim that the foundational categories of two decades of scholarship—user-generated content, networked circulation, participatory culture—are no longer empirically or analytically adequate, calling for a paradigm shift comparable in scope to the original emergence of social media studies.
